questions.question: Explain five essential services provided by an Operating System to user programs.
keyPoints: User Interface (CLI/GUI) for interaction
Program Execution - loading and running programs
I/O Operations - handling input/output devices
File System Manipulation - creating, reading, writing files
Error Detection - monitoring and reporting errors

questions.question: What is a system call? Explain the sequence of operations that occurs when a user program makes a system call.
keyPoints: System call is an interface between user space and kernel
User program invokes system call with number and parameters
Processor switches from user mode to kernel mode (mode switch)
Kernel executes the requested service
Control returns to user program with result/status

questions.question: Differentiate between API and system call with an example.
keyPoints: API is a library of functions for programmers
System call is actual request to kernel
Example: printf() is API that internally uses write() system call
API provides portability across OS; system calls are OS-specific

questions.question: Explain the fork() and exec() system calls. How are they used together?
keyPoints: fork() creates new child process - duplicates parent
fork() returns PID to parent, 0 to child, -1 on error
exec() replaces child process image with new program
Together: fork() creates process, exec() loads program into that process

questions.question: What is a file descriptor? Explain with reference to standard file descriptors.
keyPoints: File descriptor is a small non-negative integer
Used to identify open files for a process
Standard file descriptors: 0 (stdin), 1 (stdout), 2 (stderr)
Returned by open() system call; used by read(), write(), close()

questions.question: Describe any three categories of system calls with examples.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork(), exec(), wait(), exit()
File Management: open(), read(), write(), close()
Device Management: ioctl(), read(), write()
Communication: pipe(), socket(), msgget()
Protection: chmod(), umask(), chown()

questions.question: Explain in detail the various services provided by an Operating System. For each service, provide a practical example of how a user program would utilize it.
keyPoints: User Interface - CLI/GUI interaction
Program Execution - creating and running processes
I/O Operations - reading/writing to devices
File System - managing files and directories
Communication - IPC mechanisms
Error Detection - handling hardware/software errors
Resource Allocation - CPU scheduling, memory management
Protection - security and access control

questions.question: Discuss the different types of system calls used in operating systems. Give examples of each type and explain their purpose.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork, exec, wait, exit - process lifecycle
File Management: open, read, write, close, lseek - file operations
Device Management: ioctl, read, write - device interaction
Information Maintenance: getpid, getuid, sleep - system info
Communication: pipe, socket, msgget - IPC
Protection: chmod, umask, chown - security/permissions

questions.question: Explain the concept of mode switching in operating systems. How does a system call achieve this? What are the privileges of user mode versus kernel mode?
keyPoints: CPU operates in different privilege rings: user (ring 3) and kernel (ring 0)
System call triggers transition from user mode to kernel mode
Uses special instructions (syscall/sysenter) to transfer control
Kernel has access to all hardware and memory
User programs cannot directly access hardware or OS resources
Mode switch involves saving/restoring CPU state

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating the use of fork() and exec() system calls to create a child process that executes a new program.
keyPoints: Use fork() to create child process
Parent uses wait() to wait for child
Child uses exec() family (e.g., execlp) to run new program
Show proper error handling
Demonstrate parent-child relationship and program execution
